sectionDescription: High-frequency Australian IFR traps: the misconception, the actual rule, and how examiners will frame it.

categories.title: Minima, Alternates & Fuel
categories.description: Weather minima, alternate planning, and fuel requirement traps.

categories.title: PBN, GNSS & RAIM
categories.description: Navigation system, RAIM, and PBN designation traps.

modules.title: RAIM Loss Reporting Trap
modules.summary: Pilots often think any RAIM loss requires immediate ATC notification. The actual rule allows for transient outages before reporting is required.

content.text: The Misconception

Many pilots believe that any RAIM loss must be reported to ATC immediately, the moment the flag or alert appears. This leads to rushed, unnecessary calls and suggests a lack of understanding of the actual requirement.
The Actual Rule

In controlled airspace, you must advise ATC if GNSS integrity (RAIM) is unavailable for longer than 5 minutes while you are using GNSS as your primary navigation source. Brief, transient outages do not require immediate notification. The key is whether the outage is persistent enough to affect your navigation capability and ATC's separation planning.

items: Transient RAIM loss (a few seconds or a minute): confirm it is genuine, monitor, but no immediate ATC call required
RAIM loss persisting beyond approximately 5 minutes: advise ATC and state your remaining navigation capability
The 5-minute threshold reflects the point where the outage affects operational planning, not just momentary annoyance

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: You are in controlled airspace navigating on GNSS and RAIM flags for about 30 seconds then returns. What action do you take?
content.answer: I would note the outage, confirm RAIM has returned and is stable, and continue monitoring. A brief, transient RAIM flag that resolves quickly does not require an ATC call. If it had persisted beyond about 5 minutes, I would advise ATC and state my remaining navigation capability.

content.question: At what point does RAIM loss require you to advise ATC in controlled airspace?
content.answer: If RAIM is unavailable for more than about 5 minutes while I am using GNSS as my primary navigation source, I must advise ATC. The call should include what navigation capability I still have, such as reverting to VOR/DME.

content.text: Memory Hook
Five minutes to fix, then tell ATC.

refs.note: IFR rules including GNSS/RAIM reporting requirements

modules.title: Take-off vs Approach Minima Confusion
modules.summary: Take-off minima serve a completely different purpose than approach minima. Confusing the two reveals a fundamental gap in understanding.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots often think take-off minima work like approach minima: you need a certain visibility to depart just as you need a certain visibility to land. Some believe take-off minima exist so you can return and land if something goes wrong immediately after departure.
The Actual Rule

Take-off minima are primarily about obstacle clearance, not landing capability. They ensure you have sufficient visibility to see and avoid obstacles during the initial climb, or to fly a departure procedure that provides obstacle protection. The logic is fundamentally different from approach minima.

items: Approach minima: ensure you can transition from instruments to visual flight and land safely
Take-off minima: ensure you can see obstacles or fly a procedure that clears them during initial climb
You can legally depart below approach minima if take-off minima are met, even though you could not immediately return
The assumption is not that you will turn around; it is that you will continue to an alternate or wait for conditions to improve

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: The take-off minima are 1600m visibility. The approach minima for the same runway are 2400m RVR. Conditions are 1800m. Can you depart? Can you return immediately if needed?
content.answer: I can legally depart because conditions meet take-off minima. However, I cannot expect to return immediately and land on that runway, because conditions are below approach minima. Take-off minima are about obstacle clearance during departure, not about guaranteeing a return option. My planning must account for this by having a suitable alternate or waiting for conditions to improve at destination.

content.question: Why do take-off minima exist if they are not about being able to return and land?
content.answer: Take-off minima exist to ensure obstacle clearance during the initial climb phase. The visibility requirement allows you to see and avoid obstacles, or it ensures you can fly a departure procedure that provides obstacle protection. The planning assumption is that if you need to return, you will either hold for conditions to improve or divert to an alternate where conditions are suitable.

content.text: Memory Hook
Take-off is about obstacles, not options.

modules.title: "Legal But Stupid" Alternates
modules.summary: Meeting legal alternate requirements is the floor, not the ceiling. Examiners test whether you understand the difference between minimum compliance and sound judgement.

content.text: The Misconception

If the TAF shows weather at or above alternate minima at ETA, the aerodrome is a good alternate. Pilots often stop their analysis once legal requirements are satisfied, treating regulatory compliance as the end point rather than the starting point.
The Actual Rule

Legal alternate requirements set the minimum bar for planning. They do not guarantee the alternate is operationally sensible. Examiners and airline interviewers specifically test whether you can distinguish between what is legal and what is wise.

items: Legal requirements define the minimum forecast conditions for an aerodrome to count as an alternate
Operational judgement considers: trend, reliability of the TAF, single-approach aerodromes, terrain, time of day, fatigue
A TAF showing exactly minima with deteriorating trend is legal but operationally marginal
An alternate 300nm away when a closer option exists may be legal but adds unnecessary risk

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: Your planned alternate has a TAF showing conditions exactly at alternate minima with a TEMPO for conditions slightly below. The aerodrome has only one approach type. Is this a good alternate?
content.answer: It meets minimum legal requirements, but I would not call it a good alternate. The TEMPO introduces uncertainty, conditions are already at the floor, and a single-approach aerodrome offers no backup if that approach is unavailable. I would look for an alternate with better forecast margins, a second approach type, or both. Legal is the starting point, not the standard I should aim for.

content.question: How do you explain the difference between a legal alternate and a good alternate to a junior pilot?
content.answer: A legal alternate satisfies the regulatory requirements for planning purposes. A good alternate is one where I would actually feel comfortable diverting to at 2am, tired, with weather deteriorating. Legal gets you past the planning check; judgement gets you on the ground safely. I plan for the alternate I would want to use, not just the one that ticks the box.

content.text: Memory Hook
Legal gets you started; judgement gets you home.

modules.title: Part 61 IFR Recency Window Confusion
modules.summary: IFR recency is not a simple rolling 90-day window. Understanding what resets recency versus what merely maintains it is essential for legal IFR operations.

content.text: The Misconception

Many pilots believe IFR recency works like a rolling 90-day window: fly any IFR flight within the period and you remain current. Some think any instrument approach resets the clock. This oversimplification can lead to operating without valid recency.
The Actual Rule

Part 61 MOS specifies particular recency requirements for instrument rating privileges. These typically include specific tasks (approaches, holding, tracking) within defined periods. An IPC (Instrument Proficiency Check) resets your recency when it has lapsed. Flying a random IFR leg does not necessarily satisfy all recency elements.

items: Recency requirements specify particular tasks: approaches, holding, instrument time
You must complete the specified tasks within the specified period to remain current
Once recency lapses, an IPC with an approved examiner is required to restore privileges
Flying IFR does not automatically reset recency; you must complete the specific required tasks
Single-pilot IFR operations may have additional recency requirements

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: You flew an IFR sector last month but did not fly any approaches. Your last approach was 95 days ago. Are you IFR current?
content.answer: I need to check the specific recency requirements in Part 61 MOS. If the requirement includes a certain number of approaches within a defined period and I have not met that requirement, I am not current for IFR operations, regardless of having flown an IFR sector recently. The recency requirement is task-specific, not just about flying under IFR generally.

content.question: What is the difference between maintaining recency and resetting recency after it has lapsed?
content.answer: Maintaining recency means completing the required tasks within the specified period before it expires. Resetting recency after it has lapsed requires an IPC with an approved examiner. You cannot simply fly the required tasks after your recency has lapsed; the IPC is the prescribed method to restore your instrument rating privileges.

content.text: Memory Hook
Recency resets at the IPC, not with any flight.

refs.note: Flight crew licensing and instrument rating requirements
Instrument rating recency requirements and IPC standards

modules.title: Circling Minima Category Trap
modules.summary: Approach category is determined by speed, not aircraft type. Flying faster than category limits means using higher minima, regardless of what the aircraft is normally classified as.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots often assume approach category is fixed by aircraft type. A Category B aircraft is always Category B, a Category C aircraft is always Category C. They use the minima for their aircraft's nominal category without considering actual operating speed.
The Actual Rule

Approach category is based on 1.3 times Vso at maximum certificated landing weight. If your actual approach speed exceeds the upper limit for your normal category, you must use minima for the higher category. The category follows your speed, not your aircraft registration.

items: Category is defined by 1.3 Vso at maximum landing weight
If your actual speed exceeds the category limit, use the next higher category minima
Factors that increase approach speed: high landing weight, contaminated runway, ice accretion, flap limitations
You cannot use Category A minima while flying at Category B speeds
This applies to all approach minima, especially circling where the protected area varies by category

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: Your aircraft is normally Category B, but due to a flap limitation you must fly the approach at 145 knots. Which circling minima do you use?
content.answer: I would need to check whether 145 knots exceeds the Category B upper limit. If it does, I must use Category C circling minima. The category is determined by my actual approach speed, not the aircraft's nominal classification. Flying Category B minima at Category C speeds would put me outside the protected airspace.

content.question: Why does circling category matter more than straight-in category for this issue?
content.answer: For circling approaches, the category determines the size of the protected manoeuvring area. A Category C aircraft has a larger protected area than Category B, which is reflected in higher minima and wider obstacle clearance surfaces. If I fly at Category C speeds using Category B minima, I may manoeuvre outside the protected area where obstacle clearance is not guaranteed.

content.text: Memory Hook
Category follows speed, not aircraft.

refs.note: Approach category definitions and circling minima

modules.title: LSALT vs MSA Misuse
modules.summary: LSALT and MSA serve different purposes and have different buffers. Using MSA as if it were LSALT can put you uncomfortably close to terrain.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots often treat MSA as interchangeable with LSALT, assuming either provides the same level of terrain clearance for general IFR flight. Some believe MSA can be used for enroute terrain clearance whenever it appears on a chart.
The Actual Rule

LSALT (Lowest Safe Altitude) is calculated for specific routes or areas and provides terrain and obstacle clearance for enroute IFR navigation. MSA (Minimum Sector Altitude) is published for the vicinity of an aerodrome, typically within 25nm, and is intended for emergency use during approach operations. They have different clearance buffers and different intended applications.

items: LSALT: calculated for specific routes or grid areas, typically 1000ft clearance (2000ft in mountainous terrain)
MSA: published for sectors around a navigation aid or aerodrome, provides 1000ft clearance within that sector
MSA is for emergency use in the approach environment, not for enroute navigation
MSA sectors may not align with your actual track
LSALT is route-specific; MSA is point-centred

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: You are enroute, 30nm from destination, in IMC. The approach chart shows an MSA of 4500ft for your sector. Can you use this as your enroute minimum altitude?
content.answer: MSA is not designed for enroute use. It provides terrain clearance within a defined radius of the aerodrome, typically 25nm, and is intended for emergency situations in the approach environment. At 30nm I am outside the MSA coverage area. I should use the published LSALT for my route segment, or my correctly calculated LSALT if none is published.

content.question: What is the practical difference between LSALT and MSA that affects how you use them?
content.answer: LSALT is calculated specifically for the route I am flying and gives me terrain clearance along that track. MSA is centred on a point and divided into sectors; it gives me clearance within that sector but is not tailored to any specific route. LSALT is my primary enroute protection. MSA is a fallback for emergencies in the terminal area when I cannot fly a published procedure.

content.text: Memory Hook
LSALT for the route, MSA for the worst day.

refs.note: Altimetry procedures including LSALT and MSA definitions

modules.title: PBN/GNSS Failure Logic
modules.summary: GNSS failure does not automatically mean immediate diversion. The correct response depends on phase of flight, available alternate navigation, and airspace requirements.

content.text: The Misconception

When GNSS fails or RAIM is lost, pilots often believe they must immediately divert to the nearest aerodrome. This oversimplified response ignores the actual decision logic and may lead to poor choices.
The Actual Rule

GNSS failure response depends on multiple factors: phase of flight, whether you have alternate navigation capability, airspace requirements, and the nature of the failure. The response is a structured decision process, not an automatic diversion.

items: Enroute with alternate nav (VOR/DME): revert to conventional navigation, advise ATC of capability change, continue to destination if approach is available
Enroute without alternate nav: advise ATC, request vectors or assistance, consider diversion to aerodrome with conventional approach
Terminal area: assess whether you can complete a conventional approach at destination or need to divert
On GNSS approach: RAIM loss before FAF means you cannot commence; after FAF you must discontinue unless you have approved backup guidance
Controlled vs uncontrolled airspace affects reporting requirements

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: Enroute at FL250, your GNSS fails completely. You have DME/DME capability and there are VORs along your route. Destination has an ILS. Walk me through your decision process.
content.answer: First, I confirm the failure and what navigation I still have. With DME/DME and VORs available, I can continue navigating conventionally. I advise ATC of my reduced navigation capability. I check that destination has a conventional approach I can use, which it does with the ILS. There is no need for immediate diversion; I can continue to destination and fly the ILS. If destination only had GNSS approaches, I would need to consider an alternate with conventional approaches.

content.question: Your aircraft has no conventional navigation capability. GNSS fails enroute in controlled airspace. What are your options?
content.answer: This is a more serious situation. I advise ATC immediately that I have lost all navigation capability. I request radar vectors and assistance. My options are to obtain vectors to an aerodrome where I can complete a visual approach in VMC, or to request an ILS or VOR approach if ATC can vector me to final. If conditions are IMC throughout, I may need to declare an emergency to obtain priority handling and vectors to the nearest suitable aerodrome.

content.text: Memory Hook
GNSS fails? Check what you've got, then decide.

modules.title: RNP vs RNAV Naming Confusion
modules.summary: RNP and RNAV are not interchangeable terms. RNP requires onboard performance monitoring and alerting that RNAV does not. This distinction affects what procedures you can fly.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots often use RNP and RNAV interchangeably, assuming they mean the same thing. Some believe any GPS-equipped aircraft can fly any RNP procedure. This confusion can lead to flying procedures the aircraft is not approved for.
The Actual Rule

RNAV (Area Navigation) requires the aircraft to navigate within a specified accuracy. RNP (Required Navigation Performance) adds a requirement for onboard performance monitoring and alerting. The aircraft must be able to detect when it cannot meet the required performance and alert the crew. This is a fundamental difference in system capability.

items: RNAV: navigate accurately within a specified lateral tolerance
RNP: RNAV plus onboard monitoring that confirms you are meeting the required performance
RNP systems alert the crew if performance degrades below the required level
An RNAV-only aircraft cannot fly RNP procedures, even if it can navigate accurately
RNP AR (Authorization Required) procedures have additional requirements and lower minima

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: What is the fundamental difference between RNAV and RNP that affects which procedures you can fly?
content.answer: The fundamental difference is onboard performance monitoring. RNAV requires the aircraft to navigate accurately, but does not require the system to monitor and confirm that accuracy. RNP requires the system to continuously monitor its own performance and alert me if it degrades below the required level. This monitoring capability is what allows RNP procedures to have tighter tolerances and lower minima.

content.question: Your aircraft is approved for RNAV 1 but not RNP 1. Can you fly an RNP 1 approach if you can navigate accurately enough?
content.answer: No. Even if my navigation accuracy is sufficient, I cannot fly an RNP 1 approach without RNP 1 approval. The approval requires onboard performance monitoring and alerting capability, not just accurate navigation. Without that system capability, I am not approved for the procedure regardless of my actual track-keeping accuracy.

content.text: Memory Hook
RNP = RNAV plus monitoring.

modules.title: Part 91 Rules "Override" Myth
modules.summary: Part 121 or Part 135 operations do not override Part 91. Part 91 always applies as the floor. Higher Parts add requirements; they cannot relax the baseline.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots sometimes believe that operating under Part 121 or Part 135 means Part 91 no longer applies. They think the AOC operation "replaces" the general operating rules, or that company procedures can override Part 91 requirements.
The Actual Rule

Part 91 applies to all flights in Australian territory. Part 121 and Part 135 add requirements on top of Part 91; they do not replace or relax Part 91. A company operations manual can be more restrictive than Part 91 but cannot permit something Part 91 prohibits.

items: Part 91 is the regulatory floor for all operations
Part 121/135 layer additional requirements for air transport operations
If Part 91 requires something, Part 121/135 cannot waive it
Company procedures must meet or exceed Part 91 requirements
A company can prohibit circling at certain aerodromes but cannot authorise operations below Part 91 minima

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: You operate under a Part 121 AOC. A colleague suggests that Part 91 fuel requirements do not apply because Part 121 has its own fuel rules. Is this correct?
content.answer: No. Part 91 fuel requirements apply to all IFR flights. Part 121 typically adds additional fuel requirements, such as specific contingency percentages, but the Part 91 baseline always applies. Part 121 cannot reduce or waive Part 91 requirements; it can only add to them. I must comply with both Part 91 and Part 121 fuel requirements, using whichever is more restrictive.

content.question: Can a company operations manual authorise operations that Part 91 prohibits if the AOC holder believes it is safe?
content.answer: No. Company operations manuals must comply with CASR and MOS requirements. They can add restrictions beyond the regulatory baseline but cannot relax regulatory requirements. If Part 91 or the MOS prohibits something, the company cannot authorise it through internal procedures, regardless of their safety assessment.

content.text: Memory Hook
Part 91 is the floor, never the ceiling.

refs.note: General operating and flight rules applying to all operations

modules.title: Alternate Requirements with Mixed Approach Types
modules.summary: Alternate minima vary by approach type. You must consider which approach you will actually be able to fly at the alternate, not just whether any approach exists.

content.text: The Misconception

Pilots often assume that if an aerodrome has an approach and the TAF meets some alternate requirement, it qualifies as an alternate. They may not consider that different approach types have different alternate minima, or that the approach they would actually use may not be available.
The Actual Rule

Alternate minima are specified for different approach types. A precision approach (ILS) typically has lower alternate minima than a non-precision approach. You must plan based on the approach type you will actually be able to fly at your ETA, considering equipment status, NOTAMS, and your aircraft capability.

items: Precision approach alternates typically require ceiling 600ft and visibility 3200m (or as published)
Non-precision approach alternates typically require higher minima
If the ILS is NOTAMed unserviceable, you cannot use ILS alternate minima
Consider GNSS RAIM prediction for RNAV approaches at the alternate
An aerodrome may have multiple approaches but only one may be usable at your ETA

content.text: How an Examiner Will Frame It

content.question: Your alternate has an ILS and an RNAV approach. The ILS is NOTAMed unserviceable. The TAF shows conditions that meet ILS alternate minima but not RNAV alternate minima. Is this a valid alternate?
content.answer: No. With the ILS unserviceable, I must use RNAV alternate minima, which the TAF does not meet. I cannot plan on ILS alternate minima when the ILS is not available. I need to find a different alternate where the forecast meets the minima for an approach I can actually fly.

content.question: How do you determine which alternate minima to use when an aerodrome has multiple approach types?
content.answer: I use the minima for the approach I will actually fly. This means checking NOTAMs for equipment serviceability, RAIM prediction for GNSS approaches, my aircraft capability, and any other factors that might make an approach unavailable. If the only usable approach is the RNAV and the ILS is unserviceable, I apply RNAV alternate minima even if ILS minima would be lower.

content.text: Memory Hook
Match the alternate minima to the approach you'll actually fly.

refs.note: Alternate aerodrome minima by approach type
